moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the King's Indian Defense, one of the most dynamic and aggressive openings for Black. White plays 1.d4, claiming the center. The KID leads to sharp, double-edged positions where Black allows White to build a full center, then counter-attacks with ...e5 or ...f5 — creating some of chess's most exciting middlegames.
plans: Build a broad center with c4 and e4
alternatives: e4 -- leads to different openings entirely
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the King's Indian Defense, Black allows White's center then counter-attacks with ...e5 or ...f5 for sharp, double-edged play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays 1...Nf6, the Indian Defense approach — contesting central control with pieces before committing pawns. The Nf6 controls e4 and d5, preventing White from achieving an easy e4 without preparation.
plans: Play ...g6 and ...Bg7 for the King's Indian setup
Let White build the center, then attack it
alternatives: d5 -- the classical approach, meeting the center head-on
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's 1...Nf6 contests e4 and d5, preventing an easy e4 without preparation.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays c4, expanding central control alongside d4. The c4+d4 pawn duo controls d5, e5, c5, and b5 — a formidable central presence that Black will challenge from the flanks.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's c4+d4 broad center is the foundation of their strategy
plans: Play Nc3 and e4 for maximum central control
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's c4-d4 pawn duo controls d5, e5, c5, and b5, challenging Black's flank counterplay.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays g6, the King's Indian setup. The g6 pawn prepares the fianchetto of the dark-squared bishop to g7, where it will become Black's most powerful piece — controlling the long a1-h8 diagonal and supporting the eventual ...e5 central break.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's kingside fianchetto with g6 creates a fortress but gives White central space
plans: Fianchetto with Bg7, castle, then strike with ...e5 or ...c5
alternatives: d5 -- the Grunfeld, challenging the center immediately with the bishop alread...
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's g6 fianchetto aims the dark-squared bishop at the long diagonal, supporting ...e5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops Nc3, reinforcing the center and controlling d5. The Nc3 supports the e4 advance that creates White's ideal center. The KID allows White this full setup — the fight comes later.
plans: Play e4 to build the full center
The knight supports d5 and e4
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 reinforces the center and controls d5, supporting White's ideal e4 pawn center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Petrosian System (d5 without e4) is an important variation of the King's Indian Defence. The Petrosian system with d5 and Bg5 tries to restrict Black's kingside play.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the Petrosian System, d5 and Bg5 restrict Black's kingside play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Petrosian System (d5 without e4) — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Nf6 defines the Petrosian System, shaping the pawn structure and plans after d5 without e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bg7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bh4 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on g5 and moves to h4 where it serves the plan better.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bh4 improves the bishop's placement, eyeing g3 and supporting the kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nd2 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on f3 and moves to d2 where it serves the plan better.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nd2 improves the knight's placement from f3 to d2 for a better plan.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Qe8 activates the queen. From e8, the queen has maximum influence across multiple diagonals and files. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Qe8 activates the queen with maximum influence across multiple diagonals and files.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nh7. This is the typical position arising from the Petrosian System (d5 without e4). From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nh7 — typical Petrosian System position; key plans involve piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets.

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the King's Indian Defence, the Kingside Pawn Storm is a tactical pattern you should know. When White tries to stop ...f5 with g4, Black plays ...Kh8 and ...fxg4, opening the f-file.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the King's Indian, Black plays ...Kh8 and ...fxg4 to open the f-file against White's g4 pawn advance.

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the King's Indian Defence, the Central Exchange Tactic is a tactical pattern you should know. If White captures on e5 in the exchange variation, Black gets active piece play.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White captures on e5 in the King's Indian exchange variation, giving Black active piece play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the King's Indian Defence, the Ignoring the Queenside is a dangerous line you must be aware of.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the King's Indian, ignoring the queenside leaves Black's kingside attack unchecked.

moveAnnotations.annotation: cxd6 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: cxd6 opens the c-file and changes the pawn structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: cxd6 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: cxd6 opens the c-file and changes the pawn structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: In the King's Indian Defence, the Premature ...f5 Without Ne7 is a dangerous line you must be aware of.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: In the King's Indian, premature ...f5 without Ne7 leaves the king exposed.

moveAnnotations.annotation: gxf5 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: gxf5 opens lines and shifts the position's character.

moveAnnotations.annotation: gxf5 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: gxf5 opens the g-file and weakens Black's kingside pawn structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with 1.d4, the starting point for the King's Indian Defense. Black will allow White to build a broad center and then counterattack it with ...e5 or ...c5.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4 invites the King's Indian Defense: Black lets White build a broad center to counterattack with ...e5 or ...c5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, the signature first move of the King's Indian. The knight controls e4 and d5 while Black waits to see White's setup before committing pawns.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight to f6 controls e4 and d5, waiting to see White's setup before committing pawns.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays c4, establishing the classical pawn duo. Together with d4, this controls the center and prepares Nc3 to reinforce the pawn structure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's c4 and d4 pawn duo controls the center, preparing Nc3 to reinforce the structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays ...g6, entering the King's Indian setup. The fianchettoed piece on g7 will be Black's most important piece, pressuring the center along the long diagonal.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's g6 fianchetto aims the g7 bishop at the center along the long diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, reinforcing control of d5 and e4. This is the most active move, preparing to build the full classical center with e4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight to c3 reinforces d5 and e4, preparing the full classical center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto, placing the bishop on the powerful long diagonal. The g7 bishop is the soul of Black's position in the KID, exerting constant pressure toward the center and queenside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's fianchettoed g7 bishop pressures the center and queenside along the long diagonal.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays e4, establishing the imposing classical center with pawns on c4, d4, and e4.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's classical center with pawns on c4, d4, and e4 controls key squares.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays ...d6, supporting a future ...e5 advance. The pawn on d6 is a hallmark of the KID — it holds the center together and prepares the kingside counterattack.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d6 supports e5, holding the center for a kingside counterattack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the last minor piece, completing the Classical setup. The knight on f3 supports d4 and e5, and White is ready to castle and enter the middlegame.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White completes the Classical setup with knight on f3, ready to castle and support d4 and e5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, securing the king before the central clash. The king is safe behind the fianchettoed bishop, and the f8 rook will support the ...f5 pawn break.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles kingside, securing the king behind the fianchettoed bishop to support the ...f5 break.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2, a modest but solid square. The bishop supports castling and keeps options open — it can later move to f3 or d3 depending on the position.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop to e2 supports castling and can later shift to f3 or d3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays the thematic ...e5, directly challenging White's center. This is the defining move of the Classical KID — Black fights for space in the center and prepares the kingside pawn storm with .
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...e5 challenges the center and sets up the kingside pawn storm in the Classical KID.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles, getting the king to safety before the center closes. With ...e5 played, the center will likely lock after d5, and both sides will attack on opposite flanks.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White castles before the center locks after d5, shifting to opposite-flank attacks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to c6, increasing pressure on d4. This is the Classical main line move — the knight supports ...e5 and forces White to make a decision about the center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight to c6 pressures d4, supporting ...e5 and forcing White's center decision.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White closes the center with d5, the critical moment of the Classical KID. This locks the pawn structure and commits both sides to opposite-flank attacks — White pushes on the queenside (c5, b4), Bl.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d5 locks the center, committing both sides to opposite-flank attacks — White pushes queenside, Black kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to e7, heading for the kingside. From e7 it supports ...f5 and can reroute to g6 or f5 to join the attack. This is the standard regrouping in the Classical KID.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Knight retreats to e7, heading for g6 or f5 to support the kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays Ne1, the first step of the signature knight maneuver in this system. The knight leaves f3 to reroute to d3, where it will control c5 and support the b4 queenside advance.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Ne1 reroutes the knight to d3, controlling c5 and supporting the b4 queenside push.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black repositions the knight from f6 to d7, clearing the f-file for the ...f5 pawn break. The knight on d7 can later go to f6, c5, or f8 depending on how the position develops.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight retreats from f6 to d7, clearing the f-file for the ...f5 pawn break.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White completes the Ne1-d3 maneuver, placing the knight on an excellent square. From d3, it controls c5 (preventing Black's counterplay there), supports b4, and keeps an eye on f4 to slow Black's ki.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: White's knight on d3 controls c5, supports b4, and eyes f4 to slow Black's kingside play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black launches the kingside attack with ...f5, the signature KID break. This challenges White's e4 pawn and opens lines for the rooks.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...f5 challenges e4 and opens lines for the kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays f3, bolstering the e4 pawn and keeping the center closed. This is a critical defensive resource — without f3, Black's ...f4 would rip open the kingside too easily.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: f3 bolsters e4 and blocks Black's ...f4 from ripping open the kingside.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays ...f4, locking the kingside pawn structure and preparing the pawn storm with ...g5-g4. This closes the center completely, giving Black a free hand on the kingside.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...f4 locks the kingside pawns, clearing the path for a ...g5-g4 pawn storm.
